entry.title: ClangFormat
entry.content:
I use ClangFormat to format my code, it helps me to keep the code style consistent.

What is ClangFormat?

  • A code formatting tool that is part of the LLVM project.
  • Automatically formats C, C++, JavaScript, Java, Objective-C, Protobuf, and other languages.
  • Ensures consistent code style across large codebases and teams.

Benefits of Using ClangFormat?

  • Improves code readability by enforcing a consistent style.
  • Saves time and effort by automating the formatting process.
  • Reduces code review overhead by minimizing style-related discussions.
  • Supports configuration via .clang-format files, which can be versioned alongside code.

How to use ClangFormat?

1. Install ClangFormat

  • On macOS, you can install it via Homebrew : brew install clang-format
  • On Windows, you can install it via Scoop : scoop install llvm

2. Configure ClangFormat

  • Create a .clang-format file in the root directory of your project.
  • You can use clang-format -style=llvm -dump-config > .clang-format to get the default configuration file from the LLVM project, or you can create your own configuration file.
  • All the options are listed in the ClangFormat Style Options.

3. Format your code using ClangFormat

  • You can use clang-format -i <file> to format your code.
  • In VSCode, you can install the Clang-Format extension and use Ctrl+Alt+F to format your code.

My .clang-format for C/C++

Jserv’s .clang-format for C/C++

entry.title: GoogleTest Assertions
entry.content:
Here is a list of assertions that GoogleTest provides.

The majority of the macros listed below come as a pair with an EXPECT_ variant and an ASSERT_ variant. Upon failure, EXPECT_ macros generate nonfatal failures and allow the current function to continue running, while ASSERT_ macros generate fatal failures and abort the current function.

All assertion macros support streaming a custom failure message into them with the << operator, for example :

Generalized Assertion

Boolean Conditions

Binary Comparison

Does pointer equality on pointers. If used on two C strings, it tests if they are in the same memory location, not if they have the same value. Use EXPECT_STREQ to compare C strings (e.g. const char*) by value.

When comparing a pointer to NULL, use EXPECT_EQ(ptr, nullptr) instead of EXPECT_EQ(ptr, NULL).

Does pointer equality on pointers. If used on two C strings, it tests if they are in different memory locations, not if they have different values. Use EXPECT_STRNE to compare C strings (e.g. const char*) by value.

When comparing a pointer to NULL, use EXPECT_NE(ptr, nullptr) instead of EXPECT_NE(ptr, NULL).

String Comparison

The following assertions compare two C strings. To compare two string objects, use EXPECT_EQ or EXPECT_NE instead.

These assertions also accept wide C strings (wchar_t*). If a comparison of two wide strings fails, their values will be printed as UTF-8 narrow strings.

To compare a C string with NULL, use EXPECT_EQ(c_string, nullptr) or EXPECT_NE(c_string, nullptr).

Floating-Point Comparison

The following assertions compare two floating-point values.

Due to rounding errors, it is very unlikely that two floating-point values will match exactly, so EXPECT_EQ is not suitable. In general, for floating-point comparison to make sense, the user needs to carefully choose the error bound.

GoogleTest also provides assertions that use a default error bound based on Units in the Last Place (ULPs). To learn more about ULPs, see the article Comparing Floating Point Numbers.

Exception Assertions

The following assertions verify that a piece of code throws, or does not throw, an exception. Usage requires exceptions to be enabled in the build environment.

Note that the piece of code under test can be a compound statement, for example: EXPECT_NO_THROW({ int n = 5; DoSomething(&n); });

entry.title: GoogleTest Concepts
entry.content:
GoogleTest is a popular open-source unit testing framework for C++ developed by Google. It provides a rich set of assertions, test fixtures, and test discovery features that make it easier for developers to write and run tests for their C++ code.

Terminology

  • When using GoogleTest, you start by writing assertions, which are statements that check whether a condition is true. An assertion’s result can be success, nonfatal failure, or fatal failure. If a fatal failure occurs, it aborts the current function; otherwise the program continues normally.
  • Tests use assertions to verify the tested code’s behavior. If a test crashes or has a failed assertion, then it fails; otherwise it succeeds.
  • A test suite contains one or many tests. You should group your tests into test suites that reflect the structure of the tested code. When multiple tests in a test suite need to share common objects and subroutines, you can put them into a test fixture class.
  • A test program can contain multiple test suites.

Assertions

  • GoogleTest assertions are macros that resemble function calls. You test a class or function by making assertions about its behavior.
  • GoogleTest provides a collection of assertions for verifying the behavior of your code in various ways. See GoogleTest Assertions for more details.

Test Suite

  • Use the TEST() macro to define and name a test function. These are ordinary C++ functions that don’t return a value.
  • In this function, along with any valid C++ statements you want to include, use the various GoogleTest assertions to check values.
  • The test’s result is determined by the assertions; if any assertion in the test fails (either fatally or non-fatally), or if the test crashes, the entire test fails. Otherwise, it succeeds.
  • GoogleTest groups the test results by test suites, so logically related tests should be in the same test suite.

Test Fixture

  • If you find yourself writing two or more tests that operate on similar data, you can use a test fixture. This allows you to reuse the same configuration of objects for several different tests.
  • To create a fixture :
    1. Derive a class from testing::Test. Start its body with protected:, as we’ll want to access fixture members from sub-classes.
    2. Inside the class, declare any objects you plan to use.
    3. If necessary, write a default constructor or SetUp() function to prepare the objects for each test. A common mistake is to spell SetUp() as Setup() with a small u.
    4. If necessary, write a destructor or TearDown() function to release any resources you allocated in SetUp(). To learn when you should use the constructor/destructor and when you should use SetUp()/TearDown(), read the FAQ.
    5. If needed, define subroutines for your tests to share.

Run Tests

  • TEST() and TEST_F() implicitly register their tests with GoogleTest. So, unlike with many other C++ testing frameworks, you don’t have to re-list all your defined tests in order to run them.
  • Most users should not need to write their own main function and instead link with gtest_main, which defines a suitable entry point.

entry.title: GoogleTest on Windows
entry.content:
Follow the steps below to install and use GoogleTest on Windows.

Installation

  1. Go to MSYS2 Website and download the installer.
  2. Run the installer and follow the instructions.
  3. Open MSYS2 application.
  4. Install g++ using the command pacman -S mingw-w64-i686-toolchain.
  5. Install GoogleTest using the command pacman -S mingw-w64-i686-gtest.
  6. Download the GoogleTest prebuild version from here.
  7. Extract the libgtest.a and libgtest_main.a files to your project directory.

Example

Create new files named Example/add.cpp, Example/add.h, Example/add_test.cpp, you should have a directory structure similar to this:

Add the following code to add.cpp:

Add the following code to add.h:

Add the following code to add_test.cpp:

Run the following command to compile the project:

Run the following command to run the tests:

You should see the following output:

entry.title: My First Blog
entry.content:
Just finished my resume yesterday, and I’m ready to start my journey into the world of blogging.

Experience

Firmware Engineer (Nov. 2021 – Present)

  • Developed and maintained custom firmware features and motor control algorithms for electric vehicle drivers.
  • Designed and executed a comprehensive quality control strategy incorporating static analysis tools and unit test framworks, which streamlined code reviews and increased overall developer productivity by enabling quicker feedback loops for 10+ projects.
  • Self-taught in Windows desktop application development, creating various tools to improve work efficiency.
  • Integrated desktop applications, PLCs, and testing instruments to establish an automated testing platform for drivers, increasing testing efficiency by approximately 50%.

Firmware Engineer (Apr. 2020 – Nov. 2021)

  • Developed and maintained custom firmware features for Embedded Controllers (EC) in desktop and panel industrial computers.
  • Self-taught Python and created a tool that automated the generation of department documents, increasing work efficiency by approximately 80%.
  • Redesigned software design documentation and guidelines, improving clarity and consistency across the development process.

Product Sales Manager (Sep. 2019 – Dec. 2019)

  • Collaborated with senior team members to optimize product positioning and specifications.
  • Participated in competitor analysis to help the team stay informed about market dynamics.
  • Provided support in international customer service, helping to address client needs and ensure satisfaction.

Skills

  • Programming: C, C#, Python, MATLAB, PLC Ladder Diagram
  • Software Development: Unit Test (GoogleTest, CuTest, NUnit, Pytest), Static Analysis (Cppcheck, SonarQube)
  • Embedded System Development: ARM-based MCU, 8051-based MCU, UART, CAN Bus, I2C, SMBus, SPI
  • Instrument: Oscilloscope, Logic Analyzer
  • Development Tools: Git, Mercurial, IBM DOORS, Visual Studio, Eclipse, Docker
  • Design & Simulation: Simulink, LabVIEW
  • Standards: ASPICE, MISRA-C, ISO26262

entry.title: Prompt Engineering
entry.content:
Try to explore a structured approach to writing well-crafted prompts and share practices for generating high-quality content using AI.

5-Steps to write a well-structured prompt

  1. Describe the role of the AI assistant.
  2. Explain the background of your mission.
  3. Provide the task.
  4. Provide some examples of what you want.
  5. Constrain the output format.

Promt for code generation

entry.title: Reading Notes - The Elements of Style
entry.content:
This book provides clear, concise rules and principles for effective writing in English.

About the book

  • Author: William Strunk Jr.
  • Publication Date: 2018/09/27
  • Number of Pages: 239

Chapter 1 - Introductory

This book aims to give in brief space the principle requirements of plain English style.

Chapter 2 - Elementary Rules of Usage

Rule 1. Form the possessive singular of nouns by adding ’s.

  • Exceptions are the possessive of ancent proper names in -es and -is, the possessive Jesus’ and such forms for sinscience’ sake, for righteousness’ sake.

“Charles’s friend”
“John’s poems”
“the king’s malice”

Rule 2. In a series of three or more terms with a single conjunction, use a comma after each term except the last.

  • In the names of business firms the last comma is omitted as Smith, Jones and Company.

“I have visited the museums of Cairo, Alexandria, and Thebes.”

Rule 3. Enclose parenthetic expressions between commas.

  • Always to be reguared as parenthetic and to be enclosed between commas (or, at the end of the sentence, between comma and period) are the following:
    • the year, when forming part of the date
    • the day of the month, when following the day of the week
    • the abbreviations etc. and jr.[^1]
    • non-restrictive relative clauses, and similar clauses introduced by conjunctions indicating time or place. [^2]

“My siser you will be please to hear, is going to be married.” should be “My siser, you will be please to hear, is going to be married.”
[^1]: “Hank tries not to eat apples, pears, etc., even though he likes them.”
[^2]: “The students, which had at first been rather noisy, soon settled down to work.”

Rule 4. Place a comma before a conjunction introducing a co-ordinate clause.

  • Two-part sentences of which the second member is introduced by as (in the sense of because), for, or, nor, and while (in the sense of and at the same time) likewise require a comma before the conjunction.
  • If the second member is introduced by an adverb, a semicolon, not a comma, is required.
  • The uses of so (equivalent to accordingly or to so that) are somewhat colloquial and should be avoided in writing. A simple correction is to omit the word so and begin the first clause with as or since.[^3]
  • When the subject is the same for both clauses and is expressed only once, a comma is required if the connective is but.[^4]

[^3]: “I had never been in the place before; so I had difficulty in finding my way about.” should be “As I had never been in the place before, I had difficulty in finding my way about.”
[^4]: “I have heard her sing, but am still undecided about her voice.”

Rule 5. Do not join independent clauses by a comma.

  • If two or more clauses, grammatically complete and not joined by a conjunction, are to form a single compound sentence, the proper mark of punctuation is a semicolon.[^5]
  • It is of course equally correct to write the above as two sentences each, replacing the semicolon by periods.[^6]
  • If a conjunction is inserted the proper mark is a comma.[^7]
  • Note that if the second clause is preceded by an adverb, such as accordingly, besides, then, therefore, thus, and not by a conjunction, the semicolon is still required.[^8]

[^5]: “I have a dog; he is black and white.”
[^6]: “I have a dog. He is black and white.”
[^7]: “I have a dog, and he is black and white.”
[^8]: “The weather was cold; therefore, we decided to stay indoors.”

Rule 6. Do not break sentences in two.

  • In other words, do not use periods for commas.

“I met them on a liner several years ago. Coming home from London to New York.” should not be “I met them on a liner several years ago, coming home from London to New York.”

Rule 7. A participial phrase at the beginning of a sentence must refer to the grammatical subject.

“On arriving in New York, our friends led us to the hotel.” should be “When we arrived in New York, our friends led us to the hotel.”
“Young and inexperienced, the task seemed easy to me.” should be “Young and inexperienced, I thought the task easy.”

Chapter 3 - Elementary Principles of Composition

Rule 1. Make the paragraph the unit of composition: one paragraph to each topic.

  • A subject requires subdivision into topics, each of which should be made the subject of a paragraph.

Rule 2. As a rule, begin each paragraph with a topic sentence; end it in conformity with the beginning.

  • The practice here recommended enables the reader to discover the purpose of each paragraph as he begins to read it, and to retain this purpose in mind as he ends it.
  • The most generally useful kind of paragraph, particularly in exposition and argument, is that in which
    • the topic sentence comes at or near the beginning;
    • the succeeding sentences explain or establish or develop the statement made in the topic sentence;
    • the final sentence either emphasizes the thought of the topic sentence or states some important consequence.

Rule 3. Use the active voice.

  • The active voice is usually more direct and vigorous than the passive.[^9]
  • Avoid making one passive depend diectly upon another.[^10]
  • A common fault is to use as the subject of a passive construction a noun which expresses the entire action, leaving to the verb no function beyond that of completing the sentence.[^11]

[^9]: “My first visit to India will always be remembered by me.” should be “I will always remember my first visit to India.”
[^10]: “Gold was not allowed to be exported.” should be “It was not allowed to export gold.”
[^11]: “The survey of this region was made in 1900.” should be “This region was surveyed in 1900.”

Rule 4. Put statements in positive form.

  • Make difinite assertions. Avoid tame, colorless, hesitating, non-committal language.
  • Use the word not as a means of denial or in antithesis, never as a means of evasion.[^12]
  • The reader is dissatisfied with being told only what is not; he wishes to be told what is.

[^12]: “She didn’t think that studying Chinese was much use.” should be “She thought the study of Chinese useless.”

Do Don’t
The house is very old. The house is rather old.
The house is very old. The house is old.
The house is very old. The house is old.
The house is very old. The house is old.
The house is very old. The house is old.

Rule 5. Use definite, specific, concrete language.

Rule 6. Omit needless words.

Rule 7. Avoid a succession of loose sentences.

Rule 8. Express co-ordinate ideas in similar form.

Rule 9. Keep related words together.

Rule 10. In summaries, keep to one tense.

Rule 11. Place the emphatic words of a sentence at the end.
